1:
The chapter opens in the night, and the witnesses read its first verse as a study in contrast between a father who has lost his nerve and a son who has not lost his faith. Matthew Henry sets the whole scene under the providence of God, who restrained a vast Philistine army from 'falling upon that little handful of timorous trembling people that Saul had with him' — an invisible power that 'sets bounds to the malice of the church's enemies.' Against that mercy he sets the weakness of Saul, huddled with six hundred men under a pomegranate tree in the uttermost part of Gibeah, and borrows Bishop Hall's phrase for Jonathan as 'a sweet imp out of a crab-stock.' John Gill agrees the movement began by night, noting from Josephus that it was daybreak when Jonathan reached the camp; he had 'formed his scheme perhaps the night before.'

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the ground and the man. Keil and Delitzsch observe that vv.1–5 form a parenthesis of explanatory notes — Jonathan 'told not his father,' who 'would hardly have approved of so daring an enterprise' — before the thread resumes at v.6 with a repetition of his words. They place Saul at the extreme northern end of Gibeah under the pomegranate (Rimmon) by Migron. Charles Ellicott draws the portrait that governs the chapter: Jonathan is 'the typical warrior of that wild and adventurous age — recklessly brave, chivalrous, and generous,' animated by 'an intense faith in the willingness and power of the Eternal to help Israel.' That faith, he says, was 'the mainspring of the victorious power of all the great Hebrew heroes' — of Joshua and Gideon, Barak and Samson, and pre-eminently of David — 'but King Saul utterly lacked it; hence his rejection.'

Adam Clarke adds the soldier's note: Jonathan's action was 'totally contrary to the laws of war,' since no operation should be undertaken 'without the knowledge and command of the general,' yet 'it is likely that he was led to this by a Divine influence.' He lingers over the armour-bearer, tracing the word to the medieval esquire who bore the knight's lance and shield — now, 'strange to tell, a title of honour.' Beneath their differences of detail the witnesses converge on one reading: the deliverance about to unfold is God's and not Saul's, begun through a prince who was, in Henry's judgment, 'much fitter than the father to wear the crown,' and who threw himself 'into the mouth of danger, in hope of doing service to his country.' The reader is meant to feel the reproach folded into the contrast — the anointed king cowering under a tree while his uncounselled son goes out under a divine impulse to do the work of the kingdom of God.
2:
The second verse pins Saul to his tree, and the witnesses divide gently over what the tree was. John Gill keeps to the plain sense: Saul 'tarried in the uttermost part of Gibeah,' at the greatest distance from the Philistine camp, entrenched 'in the strongest part of the city.' John Wesley agrees he was 'in the outworks of the city where he had entrenched himself to observe the motion of the Philistines,' reading Migron as a place hard by Gibeah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add the muster — 'about six hundred men' — and reach forward to the priest of the next verse, identifying Ahiah, a great-grandson of Eli, with the Ahimelech afterward slain at Nob, since Ahiah ('friend of Jehovah') and Ahimelech ('friend of the King') may be two forms of one name.

The chief interest gathers on the word Rimmon. Adam Clarke notes that it 'not only signifies a pomegranate tree, but also a strong rock, in which six hundred Benjamites took shelter' (Judges 20:45), and wonders whether 'it was in this very rock that Saul and his six hundred men now lay hidden.' It is a haunting suggestion — six hundred of Saul's men cowering where six hundred of their own tribe once fled the avenging sword. Charles Ellicott declines the rock and keeps the tree, remarking on 'the love of Saul for trees, which was so common among the children of Israel,' and comparing the tamarisk under which the king is later found and the palm under which Deborah judged.

The disagreement is small, but it colours the portrait. Read as a rock, the verse deepens Saul's abjectness, hiding in the very caverns of his tribe's old shame; read as a tree, it shows a king who could still sit at ease under shade while the enemy 'ravaged his country at pleasure.' Either way the witnesses agree on the pathos of the number. Where once 'three thousand' had been chosen and trusted, only 'six hundred' remain — those whom Saul 'trusted too much' having failed him when he most needed them. The priest and the ephod are being summoned; the ark is on its way; the outward apparatus of religion is gathering around a man who has already forfeited the inward substance of it. The scene is set for a deliverance that will come from another quarter altogether.
3:
The verse names the priest at Saul's side, and the witnesses read the name as a whole history of judgment. John Gill untangles the genealogy: Ahiah is 'the son of Ahitub, Ichabod's brother,' Ichabod being the child born as Eli's house fell; Ahitub was 'an elder brother, who died young,' so that Ahiah is his son, not Ichabod's own. Adam Clarke and John Wesley both identify Ahiah with the Ahimelech of 1 Samuel 22:9, and both insist that his 'wearing an ephod' means he was 'performing the functions of the high priest' — not the plain linen garment, Wesley notes, but 'the high-priest's ephod, wherein the Urim and Thummim was,' the instrument by which the Lord was inquired of.

Charles Ellicott makes the genealogy speak. The Chronicles, 'rehearsing these facts, show us what a terrible impression the last events in Eli's reign as high priest had made in Israel' — 'the destruction of Shiloh, the death of the high priest, the fall of Phinehas and his brother in battle, the melancholy circumstances of the birth of Ichabod, were still fresh in the memory of the people.' Well might Jonathan, he reasons, 'be ready to sacrifice himself if he could deal an effectual blow upon these hereditary enemies of his country.' The disaster that had lost the ark now stands, in this priest's very lineage, at the elbow of the king who has summoned it back.

The witnesses linger over the ephod as the point of the verse. Ellicott specifies that the garment here is 'not the ordinary priestly vestment of white linen, but that official garment worn alone by the high priest, in which was the breast-plate of gems with the mysterious Urim and Thummim, by which inquiry used to be made of the Lord.' The narrator's care to note the priest and his ephod is thus not idle: it prepares the reader for the moment, later in the chapter, when Saul will call for the oracle and then wave it away, and later still when the same instrument will fall silent upon him. A house under judgment, an ephod that will not answer, a king courting the forms of a religion whose heart he has lost — the third verse quietly lays out the pieces of the drama to come.
4:
Now the ground itself becomes a character. The witnesses agree that the passes and the two crags, Bozez and Seneh, are described 'because there is no idle word in the narrative,' as Gill's method everywhere assumes. John Gill explains the topography: there was 'the passage of Michmash' and 'the passage of Gibeah,' 'in effect but one,' held by the Philistine garrison on the Michmash side, so that Jonathan's 'difficulties were very great.' John Wesley presses the geography further, correcting a natural misreading: the two rocks were not one on the right hand and one on the left, 'for so he might have gone between both'; rather 'the tooth (or prominency) of one rock was on the side, that is northward, looking towards Michmash,' and the other 'southward, looking towards Gibeah,' so that Jonathan 'was forced to climb over these two rocks, because the common ways were obstructed.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the modern survey. Between the passes 'there was a sharp rock on this side, and also one upon the other'; the one, Bozez, 'formed a pillar, a steep height towards the north opposite to Michmash,' the other, Seneh, 'towards the south opposite to Geba.' They cite Robinson's exploration of the Wady Suweinit, where side valleys enter and create the several crossings, and between them rise 'the jagged rocks Bozez and Seneh, which rose up like pillars.'

Over the names themselves the witnesses trade guesses. Adam Clarke, following the Targum, renders Bozez 'slippery' and Seneh 'treading down.' Charles Ellicott, noting that 'these names are of extreme antiquity' and 'their signification is disputed,' suggests instead that Bozez may mean 'shining' and Seneh 'the acacia,' and reports that the rocks 'have been identified by modern travellers.' The exactness is the point. By preserving the very names of two nameless-seeming crags, the sacred writer marks the spot where a nation's fortunes turned, and the commentators' care to walk the ravine with Robinson honours the same conviction: that this was a real place, and that upon its slippery pillars a real God gave a real victory through two men. Gill, Wesley, Keil, and Ellicott differ over the etymology and the exact facing of the crags, but none of them treats the detail as ornament; each labours over it because the narrator laboured over it, and the ground where God works is never merely scenery.
5:
The fifth verse completes the survey, and the two witnesses who speak on it read the geography as the frame for faith. John Gill keeps to the plain compass points: 'the northern precipice of this rock was towards Michmash, where the Philistines lay encamped,' possessing the pass, while the other faced south toward Gibeah where Saul's men lay. The verse says no more than that one crag looked north and one looked south — yet the narrator's insistence on the detail is not lost on the commentators, for it fixes exactly how steep and how guarded was the path Jonathan chose.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather the surveyed ground into its theological meaning by reading straight on into Jonathan's resolve. His purpose, they write, 'arose from the strong conviction that Israel was the nation of God, and possessed in Jehovah an omnipotent God, who would not refuse His help to His people in their conflict with the foes of His kingdom, if they would only put their whole trust in Him.' The two sharp rocks, one facing the enemy and one facing home, are not merely obstacles; they are the measure of a faith that will attempt the impossible because the God of Israel is real.

Set between the enemy's post and the king's tree, the crags dramatize the whole choice of the chapter. Behind Jonathan lies Saul, entrenched and inquiring of no one; before him rises Bozez, sheer toward Michmash, and beyond it the uncircumcised garrison. The verse that merely names directions is, in the witnesses' handling, a hinge: it turns the reader from the description of a hopeless position to the confession of a hope that rests not in the position but in the Lord. What looks, on the map, like a wall that shuts Israel in, becomes in Jonathan's hands the very stair by which God will come down to save. The geography is exact so that the miracle may be unmistakable — no ambush, no easy road, only two men climbing a guarded cliff because they believe that with Jehovah there is no restraint to save by many or by few.
6:
This is the chapter's great confession of faith, and the witnesses gather around it with one voice of admiration and several accents of emphasis. Jonathan's words — 'it may be that the LORD will work for us: for there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few' — are called by Adam Clarke 'a fine sentiment,' resting on the rule that 'where there is a promise of defence and support, the weakest, in the face of the strongest enemy, may rely on it with the utmost confidence.' John Gill notes the pastoral texture of the moment: Jonathan speaks 'a second time,' the armour-bearer having given no answer at first 'perhaps through fear,' so that the prince 'enlarges upon it for his encouragement.'

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley weighs the two halves of the saying. Jonathan calls the enemy 'uncircumcised' deliberately, 'to strengthen his faith by this consideration, that his enemies were enemies to God, whereas he was circumcised, and therefore in covenant with God, who was both able and engaged to assist his people.' Yet Wesley marks the humility of the 'it may be': 'he speaks doubtfully; for though he felt himself stirred up by God, and was assured that God would deliver his people, yet he was not certain that he would do it at this time, and in this way.' Faith here is bold but not presumptuous — sure of God, unsure only of the hour and the manner.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the theological ground hardest, and lets Bishop Hall carry the climax. Though the text does not name 'the Spirit of the Lord' as it does for Gideon or Samson, Ellicott has 'no shadow of doubt' that the same Spirit 'descended on the heroic son of Saul,' since 'all the circumstances connected with this event are evidently supernatural.' It was 'Another who would fight the armed garrison on those tall peaks.' Then Hall: 'O Divine power of faith, which makes a man more than men. The question is not what Jonathan can do, but what God can do, whose power is not in the means, but in Himself.' Beneath their several stresses the witnesses land together: the verse is not bravado but theology, and its enduring word — that God can save 'by many or by few' — leaves no believer, however outnumbered, an excuse for despair.
7:
The armour-bearer answers, and the witnesses honour the loyalty of the reply. His words — 'Do all that is in thine heart: turn thee; behold, I am with thee according to thy heart' — are read by Adam Clarke as an unreserved pledge: 'I shall accompany thee whithersoever thou goest, and share all thy dangers.' John Gill catches the promptness of it — the young man speaks 'very readily and cheerfully' — a single-hearted assent that matches Jonathan's own faith and asks no guarantees.

Charles Ellicott treasures the phrase as an authentic relic of the scene. 'The very words of the prince's armour-bearer seem to have been preserved; the expression is a colloquial one, and is rendered here literally.' It signifies, he says, simply 'Go on; I will follow.' The plainness is the beauty: no speech, no calculation, only a servant who has learned, as Matthew Henry puts it elsewhere, 'to carry not his arms only, but his heart.' Two men, one purpose, one heart — the smallest army in Scripture, and the most united.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that once the armour-bearer had 'approved of Jonathan's resolution' and stood ready to follow, Jonathan 'fixed upon a sign by which he would ascertain whether the Lord would prosper his undertaking.' The devotion of the servant is thus the hinge to the discernment of the leader: only when the two are of one heart does Jonathan turn to seek the mind of God. The witnesses draw the quiet lesson that great ventures of faith are rarely solitary. God gives Jonathan a companion whose whole reply is to lay his will alongside his master's — 'according to thy heart' — and it is in that fellowship of faith, not in numbers or arms, that the two of them go up. The verse commends the grace of a wholehearted second, the friend who will climb the cliff behind another man's conviction and count it his own. In an army of two, unity of heart is the whole strength, and Scripture pauses to record that this nameless servant supplied it without a moment's hesitation.
8:
Jonathan now unfolds his plan, and the witnesses read his boldness as itself a species of faith. 'Behold, we will pass over unto these men, and we will discover ourselves unto them.' John Gill is struck by the sheer daring of the crossing: they will go 'over the precipices to them, as steep and as cragged as they are' — no stealth by night, but an open self-disclosure by daylight to an entrenched enemy on the heights above. The plan begins not with concealment but with exposure, staking everything on the God who has stirred the venture.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the propriety of the sign Jonathan proposes. If the Philistines say, 'Wait till we come to you,' the two will stand still; if they say, 'Come up unto us,' they will go up, taking it as the token that 'Jehovah hath delivered them into our hand.' The sign, they judge, 'was well chosen': should the garrison offer to come down it would show 'some courage,' but should it dare them to climb it would betray men too cowardly 'to leave their position and attack the Hebrews.' Crucially, they insist this 'was not tempting God.' Jonathan acted 'in the exercise of his calling,' fighting 'not for personal objects, but for the kingdom of God, which the uncircumcised were threatening to annihilate,' and 'in the most confident belief that the Lord would deliver His people.'

The distinction the witnesses draw here is a fine and pastoral one. To demand a sign for one's own ends, or to fabricate a hazard and then call God to rescue us from it, would indeed be to tempt Him; but to seek His guidance within a lawful duty, when the cause is His kingdom and the trust is real, is faith reading the providence God has promised to give. Jonathan does not presume upon the outcome; he asks how he may know the will of the God he already trusts. So the verse becomes a small school of discernment: the believer may look for God's leading in the ordinary turns of circumstance, provided he is already standing where duty and covenant have set him, and provided his heart is set to follow whatever answer comes.
9:
Jonathan spells out the first half of his token: 'If they say thus unto us, Tarry until we come to you; then we will stand still in our place, and will not go up unto them.' John Gill reads this as the sign 'by which both might know how they should conduct themselves in this expedition, and what would be the issue, whether they should succeed or not.' The verse is the negative half of the discernment — the answer that would send the two men home. If the garrison offered to descend, Jonathan would take it as providence's word that the hour was not now.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the psychology the sign was built to test. A Philistine cry of 'Tarry until we come' would show that the garrison possessed enough courage to leave its post and give battle; only a taunt of 'Come up to us' would betray men so secure in their crag that they would not stir to fight two intruders. The token, therefore, was no arbitrary lottery but a shrewd reading of the enemy's temper: the very form of their insolence would disclose whether God had unnerved them.

Adam Clarke sets the moment in the great tradition of sought signs, observing that 'Jonathan had no doubt asked this as a sign from God, exactly as Eliezer the servant of Abraham did' at the well (Genesis 24:12). The comparison is illuminating and cautionary at once. Like Eliezer, Jonathan does not cast lots idly but frames a request that God, who governs the tongues of unbelieving men, may answer through their unwitting words. The witnesses thus keep two truths in tension: the sign is a genuine act of faith, appealing to a providence that reaches even into the mouths of the uncircumcised; yet it is bounded by duty, sought by a man already resolved to do the will of God whichever way the answer fell. To stand still if bidden to wait, or to climb if dared to come — either was obedience, and Jonathan's heart was ready for both, resolved beforehand to receive the enemy's answer as the voice of God.
10:
Now the sign's positive half: 'But if they say thus, Come up unto us; then we will go up: for the LORD hath delivered them into our hand.' John Gill unpacks the reasoning. A summons to 'come up,' 'however spoken in contempt,' would 'discover some fear' — men unwilling to leave their hold and expose themselves — and would besides serve to 'lead them on to make the attempt' while the garrison, careless and secure, could be surprised. The taunt Jonathan hopes to hear is thus at once a token of the enemy's overconfidence and the very opening he needs.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley plants the verse in a wider doctrine of providence. 'Jonathan, not being assured of the success of this exploit, desires a sign; and by the instinct of God's Spirit, pitches upon this. Divers such motions and extraordinary impulses there were among great and good men in ancient times.' Wesley is careful to root the confidence not in the omen but in the God behind it: 'God has the governing of the hearts and tongues of all men, even of those that know him not, and serves his own purposes by them, though they mean not so, neither do their hearts think so.'

The unanimity of the witnesses here is quietly profound. What looks like a gamble on a jeering sentry's phrase is, to faith, a resting upon the sovereignty of God over the least deliberate words of his enemies. The Philistine who will shout 'Come up' does not know he is prophesying his own defeat; he means only mockery, yet God means salvation, and the same syllables carry both. This is the doctrine that will govern the whole battle: a trembling that no man can trace, a confusion that turns each man's sword against his fellow, a rout accomplished by a power the Philistines cannot see. Jonathan's sign is the first small instance of it — the invisible hand that moves an unbeliever's tongue is the same hand that will shake the host and quake the earth. The believer may therefore commit his way to a God who is not absent even from the speech of those who deny Him.
11:
The two men step into view, and the enemy's first word is a sneer. 'Both of them discovered themselves unto the garrison of the Philistines: and the Philistines said, Behold, the Hebrews come forth out of the holes where they had hid themselves.' John Gill traces the movement: they 'passed over the valley that lay between Michmash and Gibeah, and presented themselves at the bottom of the hill or rock,' in open view, so that the garrison 'might easily discern who they were, that they were Hebrews.' The very name flung at them — Hebrews creeping from their holes — recalls the shameful hiding of 1 Samuel 13:6, when Israel had crept into caves and thickets and pits.

Charles Ellicott catches the fatal ease of the moment for the defenders. 'Easily might the sentinels of the outpost have rolled stones down the steep cliff, and hurled back the daring assailants; but they treated them with utter contempt, probably thinking to take them alive if ever they succeeded in scaling the slippery cliff.' The garrison's security is its undoing. Where a moment's real fear would have armed them with the whole weight of the mountain, their scorn disarmed them, and they let the two climbers come.

Matthew Henry, surveying the scene, hears in the taunt a word for every age of the church: 'If some of Christ's soldiers play the coward, others that play the man may perhaps be upbraided with it.' The Philistines despise the two because so many Hebrews had indeed skulked in holes; the reproach earned by the timid falls on the brave. Yet the witnesses agree that the mockery is exactly the sign Jonathan sought forming on the enemy's lips. Contempt is about to become the instrument of judgment: the men too proud to fear are the men God will overthrow. The verse thus turns on a great reversal — the derided 'Hebrews out of their holes' are the vanguard of the Lord of hosts, and the confident garrison, secure upon its crag, stands one taunt away from ruin. Faith, coming openly and unashamed, walks straight into the opening that pride has left ajar.
12:
The taunt comes exactly as hoped: 'Come up to us, and we will shew you a thing.' John Gill hears the guards 'descrying them,' calling out in challenge; Adam Clarke names it plainly 'the favourable sign which Jonathan had requested,' the Philistines meaning, 'Come, and we will show you how well fortified we are, and how able to quell all the attacks of your countrymen.' John Wesley calls the words 'a speech of contempt and derision' — the very mockery Jonathan had asked God to send.

The turn of the verse is Jonathan's answer, and the witnesses fasten on a single, telling change in his speech. Where in v.6 he had ventured only 'it may be that the LORD will work for us,' now he cries, 'Come up after me: for the LORD hath delivered them into the hand of Israel.' Keil and Delitzsch mark that in the received sign 'Jonathan received the desired token that the Lord had given the Philistines' into his hand. Wesley draws out the two graces folded into the sentence. First, its piety: 'He piously and modestly ascribes the success which he now foresees to God only.' Second, its selflessness: 'he does not say, into our hand, but into the hand of Israel; for he fought not his own glory, but the public good.'

Matthew Henry seizes the same shift as the measure of a faith now grown to full assurance. Jonathan 'had spoken with uncertainty' before; 'but now he speaks with assurance,' and 'his faith being thus strengthened, no difficulty can stand before him.' The 'it may be' of the valley has become the 'the LORD hath delivered' of the cliff, not because the odds have improved — they have not — but because God has answered, and the answered man is unstoppable. The witnesses leave two lessons standing together. Faith that begins in humble 'perhaps' is not weak faith, and it grows, upon God's confirming providence, into settled certainty. And the truly faithful heart, even in the flush of coming victory, keeps its eyes off its own name: the glory is God's, and the good is Israel's, and Jonathan is content to be only the climbing instrument of both.
13:
The climb and the first blows. 'Jonathan climbed up upon his hands and upon his feet, and his armourbearer after him: and they fell before Jonathan.' John Gill explains the posture: he 'did not attempt to go up the way the Philistines kept, but turned aside and climbed up a precipice thought inaccessible,' coming upon them 'unseen, and at unawares,' going on hands and feet because 'otherwise he could not have got up.' Adam Clarke divides the labour of the two: 'Jonathan knocked them down, and the armour-bearer despatched them.'

Charles Ellicott paints the paralysis of the guards, borrowing a vivid stroke from Dean Stanley. The few sentinels, secure in 'the inaccessibility of the craggy fortress,' first mocked, then, 'as Jonathan advanced with strange rapidity, they seem to have been, as it were, paralysed — the feat was hardly human.' Stanley recalls the tribe's weapon: 'His chief weapon was his bow; his whole tribe was a tribe of archers, and he was the chief archer of them all.' Arrived at the summit, 'in rapid succession he shot his deadly bolts,' the armour-bearer following, and twenty fell 'before they had recovered their surprise.'

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reaches past the human feat to the hidden cause. The Philistines fell because, 'being endowed with extraordinary strength and courage,' the two struck down the first with 'incredible boldness'; but more than this, 'God also struck them with a panic, and withal infatuated their minds, and possibly put an evil spirit among them, which in this universal confusion made them conceive that there was treachery among themselves,' so that they 'sheathed their swords in one another's bowels.' Here the witnesses join their two readings. The grammatical eye of Ellicott and Stanley admires the archer's speed and skill; the theological eye of Wesley traces the deeper stroke to God, who unmanned the host. Neither cancels the other: Jonathan's arm was real, and so was the terror God sent, and the twenty who fell first fell to both at once. The verse teaches that the courage of faith and the intervention of heaven are not rivals but partners — the man climbs on his hands and feet, and the Lord goes up before him.
14:
The tally of the first assault: 'about twenty men, within as it were an half acre of land, which a yoke of oxen might plow.' John Gill reads it as a measure of ferocious speed — they 'did not stop smiting, but followed their blows so quickly, that in a very little time, as well as in a very small space of ground, so many were killed.' Adam Clarke explains the odd measure: the ancients reckoned land 'by the quantum which a yoke of oxen could plough in a day,' and here it is 'probably a proverbial expression for a very small space' — the whole slaughter compressed into a plot no bigger than half a furrow.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confesses the verse's obscurity and lays out the options. The Septuagint 'either had a different text, or else translated conjecturally what they did not understand,' rendering it 'twenty men, with darts and slings and stones of the field.' Ewald reads the ploughing image vividly: Jonathan strikes down twenty at once, 'as if a yoke of land were in course of being ploughed, which must beware of offering opposition to the sharp ploughshare in the middle of its work.' The 'simplest interpretation,' Ellicott judges, is that 'twenty men were smitten down, one after the other, in the distance of half a rood of land'; Bunsen, more speculatively, thinks the verse 'an extract from a poet.'

Behind the philological debate the witnesses hold a common conviction: the smallness of the space is the greatness of the wonder. Twenty men fall in the compass of half an acre, dropped one upon another where two climbers stood — a slaughter so concentrated that only a supernatural terror can account for it, since a garrison in its right mind would simply have hemmed two men against the rock. The narrator preserves the strange measure precisely because it will not fit ordinary battle. Whether one follows the plain Hebrew, the Septuagint's slings and stones, or Ewald's ploughing simile, the effect is the same: the reader is asked to picture an impossibly crowded little killing-ground and to draw the only conclusion the chapter allows — that this was the Lord's doing, and marvellous in the eyes of two men who by every human reckoning should have died on that cliff.
15:
The panic spreads, and the witnesses agree that its name is the key to the verse: it was 'a trembling of God.' John Wesley gives the phrase its full weight — it signifies 'not only a very great trembling, but such as was supernatural, and came immediately from the hand of God. He that made the heart knows how to make it tremble.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch concur that the terror 'grew into a trembling of God, a supernatural terror miraculously infused by God into the Philistines,' seizing camp, outpost, garrison, and spoilers alike until 'the earth quaked.'

The one real division concerns the earthquake. Adam Clarke leans to metaphor: 'the trembling of the earth is probably not to be taken literally, but as a metaphor for a great commotion in the country,' though he grants 'God might have interposed and produced a real earthquake.' Charles Ellicott presses firmly the other way: 'the literal meaning is far the best.' To the panic 'an earthquake was felt, which completed the discomfiture'; the Philistines 'perceived that some Divine power was fighting against them.' He gathers the parallels: the powers of nature were summoned to Israel's aid 'as they had been before, when Pharaoh pursued the people at the Red Sea' (Exodus 14:26–27), 'as when Joshua fought the Canaanites at Beth-horon' (Joshua 10:11), and 'as when Barak smote Sisera at Kishon' (Judges 5:21).

Whether the quaking is read as literal ground or as figurative uproar, the witnesses land on one doctrine: the victory is God's alone. There was, as Matthew Henry notes elsewhere, 'no visible cause for fear' — the Philistines were numerous, bold, and well posted, and 'not an enemy made head against them but one gentleman and his man,' yet 'they shook like an aspen-leaf.' The terror had no traceable source because its source was heaven. 'Those that will not fear the eternal God,' the witnesses say with one voice, 'he can make afraid of a shadow.' The chapter's central miracle is precisely this: not a superior army, but a supernatural dread, poured into the hearts of the proud until their own confidence dissolved and their own swords turned inward.
16:
The scene shifts to Saul's watchmen at Gibeah, who see the enemy host dissolving. 'The multitude melted away, and they went on beating down one another.' John Gill sets the watchmen 'in the highest part' of hill-built Gibeah, able in the broad daylight to 'overlook the army of the Philistines' and mark the confusion. Adam Clarke reports the Vulgate's stronger reading — 'Behold the multitude were prostrate' — for 'many lay dead upon the field,' slain partly by Jonathan and his armour-bearer 'and partly by the swords of each other.'

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the obscure Hebrew of the second clause. Keil and Delitzsch report that the Rabbis read it 'magis magisque frangebatur,' the multitude 'more and more broken up,' while others take the word adverbially as 'hither and thither,' and the Septuagint renders it so but leaves part untranslated. Charles Ellicott weighs the same options and judges 'the Rabbinical interpretation the best,' noting that the watchmen, only about two miles off across a deep ravine, could plainly see the outposts in dire confusion 'rapidly spreading through the more distant camp.'

Matthew Henry, opening his survey of vv.16–23, draws the theological marrow. 'The Philistines were, by the power of God, set against one another. They melted away like snow before the sun.' The men who had held all the swords and spears of the land now found their own weapons made 'the instruments of their own destruction, and more fatal in their own hands than if they had been in the hands of Israel.' Henry hears the echo of Gideon's night and of Jehoshaphat's field, where God likewise turned an army upon itself. The witnesses agree that the verse marks the moment the miracle became visible to Israel: the watchmen see a great host destroying itself with no Israelite army in the field, and the only possible explanation is the one the chapter has pressed from the start — this is the Lord's doing. What the enemy meant as strength, their monopoly of arms, God has turned into the very means of their ruin, and the watchmen on the height are the first in Israel to read the sign.
17:
Saul, seeing the tumult but not its cause, calls the roll: 'Number now, and see who is gone from us.' Keil and Delitzsch read the king's mind: he 'conjectured at once that the excitement in the camp of the Philistines was occasioned by an attack made by Israelitish warriors,' and so ordered a muster; when it was taken, 'Jonathan and his armour-bearer were not there' — they alone were missing. Adam Clarke agrees the muster was to learn 'who and how many were absent,' Saul 'supposing that it must be by some of his own troops' that the rout had begun.

John Gill keeps to the plain sense, picturing Saul speaking 'to some of the principal officers, particularly the muster-master,' while Charles Ellicott supplies the human logic: knowing that 'he, as general-in-chief, had given no directions to any of his men to attack,' Saul 'naturally inquired as to what had caused' the panic. In 'the little Israelitish force,' Ellicott notes, 'when the roll was called, it was soon discovered who was missing.'

The witnesses find in this quiet verse a poignant reversal of stations. The king, entrenched with his six hundred, must count heads to learn that his own son has gone out to fight the Lord's battle without him; the deliverance is already under way, and Saul is the last to know its author. Where Jonathan had acted on a divine impulse he did not disclose, Saul can only react, numbering his men after the fact. The commentators do not moralize heavily here, but the shape of the story speaks: initiative in faith has passed from the anointed head to the uncommissioned son, and the father's first act in the whole affair is to discover, by subtraction, that the two bravest men in his camp had trusted God enough to leave it. The muster meant to find the missing exposes, without a word, who among them was truly present to the crisis and who had merely waited it out beneath the tree. The two who are missing are the two who believed, and the roll that names their absence unwittingly honours them.
18:
This verse hides one of the chapter's sharpest textual cruxes. The Hebrew has Saul say, 'Bring hither the ark of God,' with the note that 'the ark of God was at that time with the children of Israel'; the Septuagint reads instead, 'Bring hither the ephod,' since Ahiah 'bore the ephod in those days before the children of Israel.' John Gill already leans toward the ephod, glossing that Saul wanted the priest 'to inquire by the Urim and Thummim,' and citing Kimchi to the same effect. Adam Clarke reads the intent simply: Saul 'wished to inquire what use he should make of the present favourable circumstances.'

The grammatical-historical witnesses press the difficulty hard. Keil and Delitzsch are 'struck' by the reading, since 'the ark was then deposited at Kirjath-jearim, and it is very improbable it should have been in the little camp of Saul,' and because elsewhere inquiry is made 'not of the ark, but of the ephod.' Charles Ellicott mounts the fullest case: the Septuagint's 'ephod' yields a plain sense, for 'no question or oracle could be asked of the Ark,' the Urim and Thummim being 'connected, not with the Ark, but with the high-priestly ephod'; Josephus too 'had before him a text corresponding to the LXX.' Ellicott concludes 'the reading of the LXX probably represents the original Hebrew,' the clause about the ark being 'an explanatory gloss of some ancient scribe.'

Yet the witnesses record a dissent worth weighing. Maurer, cited by Ellicott, 'prefers the present Hebrew text,' arguing that 'at that supreme moment of danger Saul wanted not the advice of an oracle, but rather the help and encouragement which the presence of the sacred Ark would give.' Ellicott himself resists this: it 'would rather degrade Saul to the level of the superstitious Hophni and Phinehas,' who had carried the ark to ruin, whereas Saul, 'with all his faults, was a far nobler type of man than those profligate priests.' The debate leaves the pastoral point untouched. Whether Saul reached for oracle or for talisman, the verse shows a king turning at last, and belatedly, toward God — a movement about to be aborted in the very next breath. The apparatus of inquiry is summoned; the question is whether the heart behind it will wait for an answer.
19:
Saul's turn toward God is over before it begins: 'Withdraw thine hand.' Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew literally — 'draw thy hand in, back; leave off now' — for as the tumult 'became greater and greater,' Saul 'saw from that what ought to be done, and stopped the priest's inquiring of God.' Adam Clarke reads it charitably as a soldier's instinct: 'before he had made an end of consulting,' the swelling noise 'called his attention,' and 'finding there was no time to lose, he immediately collected his men.' John Wesley puts the words in Saul's own mouth: 'Trouble not thyself to inquire, for I now plainly discern the matter.'

Charles Ellicott is less indulgent, and he lets the old bishops preach. 'The instinct of the general soon got the better of his first desire for some Divine guidance'; the hour was come 'to fight rather than to pray.' He quotes Bishop Andrewes: 'There are some who with Saul will call for the Ark, and will presently cry, Away with it — that is, will begin their prayers, and break them off in the midst on every occasion.' And Bishop Hall: 'Saul will consult the Ark; hypocrites, when they have leisure, will perhaps be holy. But the Ark must give place to arms.'

Matthew Henry draws the same edge. Saul 'was in such haste to fight a falling enemy that he would not stay to make an end of his devotions, nor hear what answer God would give him.' Henry finds the deeper fault beneath the haste: 'the more evident it was that God did all, the more reason Saul had to inquire whether God would give him leave to do anything,' yet 'a little thing will divert a vain and carnal mind from religious exercises.' His verdict is the chapter's proverb: 'He that believeth will not make haste, such haste as this, nor reckon any business so urgent as not to allow time to take God along with him.' The witnesses converge on a portrait of piety that is real but shallow — quick to reach for the oracle when danger threatens, quicker still to silence it when opportunity beckons. Saul's half-finished prayer becomes a mirror for every hurried soul that calls on God and will not wait for Him to speak.
20:
Saul at last takes the field, and finds the work already done. 'Saul and all the people assembled themselves, and behold, every man's sword was against his fellow.' Charles Ellicott examines the verb: the margin's 'were cried together' means 'assembled by the trumpet call,' but the Syriac and Vulgate more accurately render it 'shouted' — Israel 'raised the war-cry.' Shouting 'the well-known war-cry of Benjamin,' the small but disciplined band 'penetrated wedge-like into the heart of the broken Philistine host.' Keil and Delitzsch confirm that Saul 'had himself and all the people called together for battle,' and that the mutual slaughter arose 'partly of terror, and partly of the circumstance' the next verse will name.

John Gill pauses over the numbers, wondering whether Saul's six hundred were joined again by the thousand once posted at Gibeah, but the witnesses agree the arithmetic hardly matters, for the decisive stroke was not Saul's. The Philistines, who had held every sword and spear in the land, were now cutting one another down — the monopoly of arms turned into a civil massacre by the terror of God.

Matthew Henry, surveying the rout, presses the irony to its Gospel point. Israel, he observes, 'seem bold and brave when the work is done to their hands.' 'Our Lord Jesus,' he writes, 'had conquered our spiritual enemies, routed and dispersed them, so that we are cowards indeed if we will not stand to our arms when it is only to pursue the victory and to divide the spoil.' The commentators thus read the verse on two levels at once. Historically, it is the moment Saul's forces enter a battle already won, joining a slaughter God had begun through two men. Typologically, it becomes a figure of the church's warfare: the field is not level, the enemy is already broken by a victory not our own, and the believer is called not to win the day but to follow up a triumph Christ has secured. To hang back now, when the swords of the foe are turned upon themselves, would be shameful cowardice; the least the redeemed can do is press the pursuit.
21:
The tide turns visibly as Israel's own turncoats come home. 'The Hebrews that were with the Philistines before that time turned to be with the Israelites that were with Saul and Jonathan.' John Gill canvasses who these men were: 'servants and bondsmen,' or conscripts 'forced into their army,' or perhaps 'renegades from the Israelites, deserters, who for safety and subsistence betook themselves to them as the stronger party.' Charles Ellicott narrows it to 'prisoners who had been compelled to fight against their countrymen, or levies raised in those parts more immediately under Philistine influence' — men who 'took the first opportunity to go over to Saul.'

A small textual and grammatical debate attends the verse. Adam Clarke notes that 'instead of Hebrews the Septuagint read the slaves,' having found 'abadim, servants' where the Hebrew has 'Ibrim, Hebrews,' though 'this reading is not countenanced by any other version.' Keil and Delitzsch defend the received text and explain the change of names theologically: the Israelites in the Philistine ranks are called 'Hebrews, according to the name current among foreigners,' while those with Saul are called 'Israel, according to the sacred name of the nation.' The verb 'turned,' they add, the older versions fill out as 'returned' — Chaldee, Septuagint, Vulgate, Syriac, and Arabic alike supplying the sense the terse Hebrew leaves implicit.

Beneath the philology the witnesses find a study in the fickleness of the crowd and the drawing power of a rising cause. John Wesley reads the men as having gone to the Philistines 'either by constraint, as servants, or in policy, to gain their favour and protection' — and now, seeing the wind change, coming back. The commentators do not flatter these returners; their loyalty follows the fortunes of the day. Yet the verse also carries a gentler note: God's victory reclaims even the compromised and the coerced. Israelites who had marched, willing or unwilling, under the enemy's banner are gathered again to 'Saul and Jonathan,' the sacred name restored to them. The deliverance that began with two faithful men widens to draw home the many who had drifted, and the nation that had scattered into holes and into servitude is knit together again around the battle of the Lord.
22:
The hidden come out of hiding. 'All the men of Israel which had hid themselves in Mount Ephraim, when they heard that the Philistines fled, even they also followed hard after them.' John Gill recalls where they had been — 'in the caves and rocks, thickets and pits' of the earlier terror (1 Samuel 13:6). Keil and Delitzsch trace the sweep of the pursuit: 'the Lord helped Israel that day, and the conflict went out beyond Bethaven,' the battle spreading from Michmash westward, the fleeing enemy pursued at last 'as far as Ajalon.'

Adam Clarke notes a textual addition: 'the Vulgate and the Septuagint add here, And there were with Saul about ten thousand men, but this is supported by no other authority.' The number, whether original or not, marks how swiftly the little band swelled once the rout was plain, as the fearful crept from their holes to join a victory they had done nothing to win.

Matthew Henry reads these late-comers with a candour that is neither harsh nor flattering. 'Those that had fled their colours and hid themselves in the mountains returned to their posts, and joined in with the pursuers, hoping by their great zeal and officiousness, now that the danger was over and the victory sure, to atone for their former cowardice.' It was, he grants, 'not much to their praise to appear now,' yet 'it would have been more their reproach if they had not appeared'; for 'those are remiss and faint-hearted indeed that will not act in the cause of God when they see it victorious as well as righteous.' The witnesses thus hold the verse in a fine balance. There is no glory in the courage that waits for the danger to pass; the men of Mount Ephraim are no Jonathans. But there is a real duty in joining the Lord's cause once its triumph is manifest, and to refuse even that would be the deeper shame. God's victory has room in it for the belated and the timid, provided they come at all — and the whole scattered nation is drawn, wave upon wave, into the deliverance two men began.
23:
The narrator seals the day with a single confession: 'So the LORD saved Israel that day.' John Gill dwells on the wonder and the grace of it: 'a wonderful salvation it was, that two men should throw such a vast army into confusion.' This 'could only be of the Lord,' and — Gill presses the point — it was 'a free favour bestowed on an undeserving prince, who had behaved ill to his prophet at Gilgal, and now to him and his high priest at Gibeah.' The salvation is sovereign and unmerited, poured out over a king who has forfeited every claim to it.

Charles Ellicott hears the deliberate echo of an earlier redemption: these are 'the identical words used at the Red Sea, after the deliverance of the people from Egypt.' The battle 'rolled westward through Beth-aven, past city and village, over Mount Ephraim' — 'a decisive victory, crushing in its results to the Philistines, who were driven back so effectually as not to re-appear till the close of Saul's reign.' The verse that credits God with the whole is also the verse that opens Saul's years of military success.

John Wesley and Matthew Henry guard the balance the verse itself strikes. Wesley notes that though 'the warriors who were engaged in the battle' did the pursuing, still 'it is said, the Lord saved Israel that day: he did it by them; for without him they could do nothing. Salvation is of the Lord.' Henry says the same: God 'did it by them, for without him they could do nothing.' The witnesses land, with one voice, on the doctrine that governs the whole chapter and echoes across all Scripture — that deliverance is God's work through human hands, never the reverse. Two climbing men, a shouted war-cry, returning turncoats, and emerging cowards were all real instruments; but the honour belongs to the Lord who wielded them. The Red Sea and Michmash speak the same word: it is the Lord who saves, and He is pleased to save both by many and by few. The chapter that opened with a lonely act of faith closes its first movement here, with the whole glory laid, without remainder, at the feet of God.
24:
The triumph curdles into a self-inflicted distress, and the witnesses are nearly unanimous in blaming Saul's rash oath. 'Cursed be the man that eateth any food until evening, that I may be avenged on mine enemies.' Keil and Delitzsch read the vow as the pivot of the chapter's second half: it 'did not proceed from a proper attitude towards the Lord, but was an act of false zeal, in which Saul had more regard to himself and his own kingly power than to the cause of the kingdom of Jehovah,' betrayed by the phrase 'till I have avenged myself upon mine enemies.' It was, they conclude, 'a despotic measure.'

Matthew Henry anatomizes the fault under three heads. The order was 'impolitic and very unwise, for if it gained time it lost strength for the pursuit'; it was 'imperious, and disobliging to the people,' worse than 'muzzling the mouth of the ox that treads out the corn'; and above all it was 'impious to enforce the prohibition with a curse and an oath.' 'Had he no penalty less than an anathema?' Henry asks; 'though superiors may chide and correct, they may not curse their inferiors; our rule is, Bless, and curse not.' He even suspects that when David later 'speaks of an enemy that loved cursing, perhaps he meant Saul' (Psalm 109:17–18). Adam Clarke adds the tactical folly: the fast was 'the very means of defeating his own intention, for as the people were exhausted for want of food, they could not continue the pursuit.'

The one voice of partial defence is John Wesley, who allows that 'Saul's intention was good, and the matter of the obligation was not simply unlawful — if it had not been so rigorous in excluding all food, and in obliging the people under pain of an accursed death, a punishment far exceeding the fault.' John Gill concurs that the command 'would not have been so much amiss, had he not annexed a curse to it.' So the witnesses draw a careful line. Zeal against the enemy is not condemned; a fast in war might be lawful; but Saul's vow was ruined by its rashness, its self-regard, and its cruelty — a curse that would recoil, before the day was out, upon his own beloved son. The chapter's great deliverance and its great folly stand side by side, that the reader may learn how quickly a leader's unbridled will can turn a victory God gave into a snare for his own people.
25:
The pursuit leads into a wood dripping with honey, and the witnesses set the scene as a picture of Canaan's own bounty offered and refused. 'All they of the land came to a wood; and there was honey upon the ground.' John Gill clarifies that 'all they of the land' means not the whole nation but 'all that came with Saul and Jonathan, and joined them in the pursuit.' Keil and Delitzsch describe the terrain precisely: the Hebrew signifies 'a woody district, in which forests alternated with tracts of arable land and meadows,' and there, upon the open field, lay honey.

Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott naturalize the marvel. Clarke notes 'there were many wild bees in that country, and Judea is expressly said to be a land flowing with milk and honey.' Ellicott fills in the mechanics: in 'the wilder parts of the land the old woods were not yet cleared,' and 'the wild bees fill the hollow trees with honey, till the combs, breaking with the weight, let the honey run down upon the ground.' The exhausted army walks through a forest where the very promise of the land — milk and honey — lies pooling at their feet.

The irony the witnesses feel here is deliberate and sharp. The soldiers pass through a place that embodies the covenant blessing, the sweetness God had sworn to give His people, and they may not touch it — not because God forbids it, but because a man has laid a curse across it. The land flows with honey, and Saul's oath dams the stream. What God spreads freely, the king's rash zeal turns into a forbidden thing, and the very bounty meant to strengthen Israel becomes an occasion of faintness and snare. The verse quietly sets the divine generosity against the human severity that will dominate the rest of the chapter: heaven pours out honey upon the ground, and the anointed head decrees that the hungry shall pass it by. Gill, Keil, Clarke, and Ellicott differ only in the mechanics of the marvel; on its meaning they are one, for the forest of honey is Canaan itself, and the fast is a shadow the king has cast across the promise.
26:
The honey drips before their eyes, and not a hand is raised. 'When the people came into the wood, behold, the honey dropped; but no man put his hand to his mouth: for the people feared the oath.' John Gill speculates on its source — from trees that 'produce honey,' from combs framed by bees, or from hives whose combs the fleeing Philistines had crushed, 'perhaps pressed as they fled.' Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott both render the Hebrew vividly as 'a stream of honey,' while Adam Clarke compares the 'honey dews' he has himself seen 'in considerable quantity on the trees and long grass.' John Wesley notes that bees 'make their hives in the trunks of trees, or clefts of rocks, or holes of the earth, and this eminently in Canaan.'

The naturalists agree on the wonder; Matthew Henry presses it into a moral. The soldiers, he says, 'sucked honey out of the rock, the flinty rock' (Deuteronomy 32:13), 'yet, for fear of the curse, they did not so much as taste.' From their restraint he draws a searching commendation: 'Those are worthy of the name of Israelites that can deny themselves and their own appetites, even when they are most craving, and the delights of sense most tempting, for fear of guilt and a curse.'

Yet the witnesses hold this praise in tension with the chapter's larger verdict. Henry's own conclusion — 'Let us never feed ourselves, much less feast ourselves, without fear' — honours the self-denial of the men even while the narrative condemns the oath that demanded it. The soldiers' discipline is genuinely admirable: hungry, spent, and walking through streaming sweetness, they will not break a vow they believe binds them. But their very obedience exposes the tyranny under which they stand, for it is a good instinct enslaved to a bad command. The verse thus works on the reader two ways at once. It rebukes the greedy soul that cannot pass by a forbidden pleasure; and it grieves over the needless burden a rash ruler has laid on faithful men, who honour their king's word to their own hurt while the honey God provided runs untasted at their feet.
27:
Only Jonathan, out of earshot of the vow, tastes the honey. 'He put forth the end of the rod that was in his hand, and dipped it in an honeycomb, and put his hand to his mouth; and his eyes were enlightened.' John Gill stresses the innocent ignorance: Jonathan 'heard not' the charge, being off 'either fighting or pursuing' when it was given. Charles Ellicott agrees he 'had heard nothing of his father's rash oath, and was, owing to his exertions, worn out with fatigue and hunger,' so that 'with the point of his staff' he 'took up a piece of the honeycomb.'

The witnesses fasten on the enlightened eyes as a small physiological wonder with a spiritual glow. Adam Clarke observes plainly that 'hunger and fatigue affect and dim the sight; on taking food, this affection is immediately removed.' Keil and Delitzsch read the Hebrew as 'his eyes became seeing,' his 'lost strength, which is reflected in the eye, having been brought back by this invigorating taste.' John Wesley sums it: 'he was refreshed, and recovered his lost spirits; this cleared his sight, which was grown dim by hunger and faintness.'

Charles Ellicott crowns the note with the rabbis' own gloss. The Talmud, treatise Yoma, draws from this very verse a rule of health: 'Whoever suffers from the effects of intense hunger, let him eat honey and other sweet things, for such eatables are efficacious in restoring the light of one's eyes,' citing Jonathan's brightened gaze as proof. The witnesses let the small scene stand as both fact and figure. On its surface it is a hungry soldier's brief refreshment, a spoonful of wild sweetness that lifts the fog of exhaustion from a brave man's eyes. Beneath, it is the innocent act that the chapter's machinery will turn into a capital charge — for the very taste that opened Jonathan's eyes will, by his father's oath, be laid to his account as a sin worthy of death. The narrator lets the reader see how enlightening and how harmless the deed was, precisely so that the coming sentence may appear as monstrous as it is.
28:
A bystander breaks the news of the oath, and the honey turns bitter in the telling. 'Thy father straitly charged the people with an oath, saying, Cursed be the man that eateth any food this day. And the people were faint.' Charles Ellicott makes a small but telling grammatical correction: the closing words 'should be rendered, and the people are faint — part of the speech of the soldier who was telling Jonathan of his father's rash oath.' The faintness is not a narrator's aside but the soldier's own complaint, laid at the oath's door.

Keil and Delitzsch record Jonathan's immediate verdict, which the next verse will voice: told of the oath 'in consequence of which the people were exhausted,' Jonathan 'condemned the prohibition,' saying his father had 'brought the land into trouble.' John Gill pictures the exchange concretely: Jonathan, refreshed, may well have been 'directing and encouraging the people to do as he had done,' at least by his example, when 'one of the men that came along with Saul' overheard and 'replied' with the warning. The soldier's word does two things at once — it clears Jonathan of willful disobedience, since he plainly knew nothing, and it names the true cause of the army's weakness.

The witnesses feel the dramatic irony of the moment keenly. Jonathan, whose eyes have just been opened by a taste of honey, now has his eyes opened to a graver thing: that his own act, wholly innocent, has fallen under a curse he never heard pronounced. The bystander stands for the whole army's plight — faint, forbidden, and bound by an oath most of them dread more than they understand. And the contrast the chapter has been building sharpens here to a point. The father's word spreads faintness and fear through the ranks; the son's instinct would spread strength and cheer. One taste of honey has more life in it than the king's whole edict, and the plain soldier who reports the oath does so with a weariness that is itself an indictment. The scene is set for Jonathan's open protest, and for the reader to weigh whose zeal has served Israel and whose has troubled it.
29:
Jonathan speaks his mind: 'My father hath troubled the land.' The witnesses agree on the substance of his charge but divide sharply over its propriety. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott take his side on the merits. Ellicott paraphrases: 'My father's ill-considered vow has done grave harm to us in Israel. Had he not weakened the people, our victory would have been far more complete. Utter exhaustion has prevented us from following up our victory.' The complaint, on this reading, is simply true — the oath has cost Israel the fruits of the day.

John Gill, however, will not let the sentiment pass unrebuked, even while granting its truth. 'This was not wisely said by Jonathan,' he judges; 'how much soever his father was to be blamed, it did not become him as a son thus to reflect upon him, and it might have tended to mutiny and sedition.' Gill notes the Targum reads it as 'afflicted and distressed' the people, 'made them uneasy, like troubled waters,' and the Arabic even as 'my father hath sinned against the people.' The word Jonathan uses is strong; the son who speaks it, however justified, risks undermining the very authority he serves.

The debate the witnesses stage here is a genuine and instructive one. Was Jonathan's outburst righteous candour or filial imprudence? Ellicott and Keil hear a brave man naming a plain harm; Gill hears a son publicly shaming a father before the troops, whatever the provocation. Both, notably, agree that Saul is truly at fault — the difference is only whether Jonathan was the one to say so, and so loudly. The tension is not resolved by the text, and the witnesses are content to leave it standing, for it presses a real question upon every reader who must live under flawed authority. There is a time to speak the truth about a leader's folly, and there is a way of speaking it that heals rather than inflames. Jonathan's words are vindicated by events; whether they were becoming in a son and a soldier, the commentators leave the reader to weigh.
30:
Jonathan completes his protest with an argument from the greater to the lesser. 'How much more, if haply the people had eaten freely today of the spoil of their enemies which they found? for had there not been now a much greater slaughter among the Philistines?' Only John Gill comments at length, and he draws out the force of the reasoning: 'had they been allowed to eat freely of the provisions, of bread, wine, and the like, they found in the enemy's camp, they would have been much more refreshed and strengthened.' The inference is plain — 'if a little honey had had such an effect upon him, of what service would a full meal have been to the people?'

The verse rests upon a simple, undeniable logic, and the witnesses let it stand as the crown of Jonathan's case. His own experience is the evidence: a mere touch of honey on a rod's end had cleared his eyes and revived his spirits. If that trifle could restore one weary man, then a proper meal, taken from the abundant Philistine spoil, could have carried the whole army through the pursuit with strength to spare. Saul's oath had denied them exactly this, and the denial had bled away the victory. The 'much greater slaughter' that never happened is Jonathan's measure of the loss.

What the commentators find in the verse is the practical wisdom that Saul's zeal lacked. Matthew Henry, expounding these verses together, had already reasoned to the same end: 'It is bread that strengthens man's heart; therefore Jonathan reasoned very well, If the people had eaten freely, there would have been a much greater slaughter.' Daily work, Henry adds, 'cannot be done without daily bread, which our Father in heaven graciously gives us.' The witnesses thus turn Jonathan's complaint into a doctrine of stewardship over the body: God gives food to sustain His servants for His work, and to withhold it in the name of devotion is not piety but folly. Saul had confused severity with sanctity; Jonathan, the truer soldier, saw that a fed army would have honoured God more by finishing the rout than a fasting one could by keeping a needless vow.
31:
The narrator measures the day's pursuit and its cost in a single breath. 'They smote the Philistines that day from Michmash to Aijalon: and the people were very faint.' John Gill traces the line from 'where the camp of the Philistines was' to Aijalon, which he takes, following Bunting, as 'twelve miles from Michmash.' Charles Ellicott reckons the distance greater — Aijalon, 'the modern Yalo, is some eighteen or twenty miles from Michmash' — so that 'the battle and pursuit had then extended some twenty miles of country.' Adam Clarke, citing Calmet, gives it as 'three or four leagues.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix Aijalon at the village of Yalo and note it lay 'about three geographical miles to the south-west of Michmash,' the fleeing enemy driven the whole way. But the witnesses agree that the verse's real weight falls on its last clause: 'the people were very faint.' Keil adds the plain cause — 'because Saul had forbidden them to eat before the evening.' The scale of the victory and the depth of the exhaustion are set side by side deliberately.

The commentators let the two halves of the verse indict Saul's oath by their mere juxtaposition. Israel chased the Philistines across some twenty miles of hill country, a rout of impressive reach; yet at the end of that long pursuit the pursuers could scarcely stand. The very success of the day exposed the folly of the fast, for a stronger army might have turned a rout into annihilation, and a fed one would not have collapsed at the finish. The 'much greater slaughter' Jonathan had imagined is thrown into relief against the faintness the narrator records. Here the chapter's second movement reaches its low ebb: victory won, opportunity squandered, and a whole army stumbling on empty toward the evening when, at last released from the curse, they will fall upon the spoil in a way that turns their hunger into fresh sin. The commentators quarrel a little over the miles, but the point of the verse is the joining of two facts the oath forced together — a great pursuit and a fainting people.
32:
Released at evening, the famished army falls on the plunder and sins in its haste. 'The people flew upon the spoil, and took sheep, and oxen, and calves, and slew them on the ground: and the people did eat them with the blood.' John Gill hears the violence in the verb: they 'flew upon the spoil like a swift and ravenous bird,' and 'with the greatest haste and eagerness laid hold on what came first to hand.' Adam Clarke names the transgression and its cause together: 'they were faint through hunger, and did not take time to bleed the cattle on which they fed. This was another bad effect of Saul's rash adjuration.'

Charles Ellicott supplies a vivid comparison from Dean Stanley: 'The moment that the day, with its enforced fast, was over, they flew, like Mussulmans at sunset during the fast of Ramazan, upon the captured cattle, and devoured them, even to the brutal neglect of the Law forbidding the eating of flesh which contained blood.' The reference is to the standing prohibition of Leviticus 17:10–14 and Leviticus 19:26, that Israel must not eat flesh with the blood. Two hungry meals, as the proverb Henry cites has it, make the third a glutton.

John Wesley draws the moral the witnesses all feel pressing: 'So they who made conscience of the king's commandment for fear of the curse, make no scruple of transgressing God's command.' Here is the deepest irony of Saul's oath. The men who would not so much as taste honey, in dread of a human anathema, now gulp down blood in defiance of a divine statute — the fear of man had bound them where the fear of God had not. Keil confirms the picture from the Hebrew, the animals felled 'to the earth' so that the blood was not drained but eaten upon. The commentators trace the whole disorder back to Saul: his ill-judged fast, meant to sharpen Israel's zeal, has instead starved them into open violation of the Law of God. A curse laid to serve religion has become the very engine of transgression, and the king who forbade a lawful bite must now confront a lawless feast of his own making.
33:
Word reaches Saul, and he moves at once to stop the sin. 'Behold, the people sin against the LORD, in that they eat with the blood. And he said, Ye have transgressed: roll a great stone unto me this day.' Keil and Delitzsch render his rebuke sharply — 'Ye act faithlessly towards Jehovah' by breaking the covenant law — and read the great stone as a place where the beasts might be slain properly, 'so that the blood could run off upon the ground, and the flesh be separated from the blood.' Adam Clarke takes the stone as an improvised altar, 'on which the animals might be properly slain, and the blood poured out upon the earth.'

The witnesses divide over how to read Saul's sudden scrupulosity. John Wesley lays bare the blind spot: 'He sees their fault, but not his own, in giving the occasion of it.' The king who caused the transgression by his fast now upbraids the transgressors, never suspecting himself. Charles Ellicott makes this the very key to Saul's character. His care that 'the Law of Moses should be kept and honoured, even under circumstances of the direst necessity,' shows him 'a strange complex character — now superstitiously watchful lest the letter of the Law should be broken; now recklessly careless whether or not the most solemn commands of God were executed.'

The commentators let the two sides of Saul stand together without softening the contradiction. On the one hand his instinct is right and even admirable: a lesser leader might have let famished troops eat as they pleased, but Saul halts the whole army to see the Law obeyed. On the other hand the scene is heavy with self-ignorance, for the very disorder he corrects is the child of his own rash oath. Ellicott's phrase captures the man exactly — scrupulous over the letter, careless of the weightier matter. Saul can enforce a ritual detail with zeal and yet remain wholly unhumbled about the pride and cruelty that produced the crisis. It is the portrait of a piety that strains at gnats it can see in others while swallowing the camel of its own will, and the witnesses offer it as a warning against a religion of the surface that never turns its scrutiny inward.
34:
Saul's remedy is put into swift, orderly effect. 'Disperse yourselves among the people, and say unto them, Bring me hither every man his ox, and every man his sheep, and slay them here, and eat; and sin not against the LORD in eating with the blood.' John Gill pictures the king sending 'some one way, and some another,' as heralds 'to publish his will and pleasure' through the camp. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the practical purpose: each beast was to be slain at the great stone, 'so that the blood could run off properly upon the ground, and the flesh be separated from the blood,' and 'this the people also did.'

The witnesses note how readily the disorder is cured once the leader acts. The same famished crowd that a moment before 'flew upon the spoil' now brings, 'every man his ox with him that night,' to be slaughtered lawfully under the king's eye. Matthew Henry draws the lesson that governs the verse: 'so easily were they restrained and reformed when their prince took care to do his part.' From this he presses a maxim for all in authority: 'If magistrates would but use their power as they might, people would be made better than they are, with more ease than is imagined.'

Here the commentators grant Saul his due, and it is worth marking. The king who caused the crisis by his rash oath now shows what rightful authority can do when it turns to its proper work: a single decisive order stops a whole army from sinning, and the people obey without resistance. The blood is drained, the Law is kept, and the transgression halts as quickly as it began. Henry's point is not sentimental; it is a sober confidence in the ministry of good government to restrain evil and foster obedience. The tragedy of Saul is precisely that he could do this — he was capable of leading Israel into order and reverence — and yet his own unbridled will kept undoing the good his office might have wrought. The verse shows the king at his best, reforming his people by simply doing his part, and thereby measures how far his worst falls short of it.
35:
The stone of slaughter becomes an altar. 'Saul built an altar unto the LORD: the same was the first altar that he built.' John Gill takes it as raised 'to offer peace offerings, in thankfulness for the victory,' and perhaps sin offerings too, 'to make atonement.' Adam Clarke reads the note about its being his first altar in the light of Saul's earlier presumption: 'Samuel, as prophet, had hitherto erected the altars, and Saul thought he had sufficient authority to erect one himself without the prophet, as he once offered sacrifice without him.'

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the Jewish debate over the odd Hebrew phrase. Abarbanel reads that 'Saul began to build, but did not finish'; the Midrash prefers that 'Saul began among the kings of Israel the building of altars'; but the 'more obvious meaning,' Ellicott judges, is 'that this was the first public acknowledgment King Saul made to God for the mercies vouchsafed to him.' Keil and Delitzsch add that the altar 'was probably not intended to serve as a place of sacrifice, but simply to be a memorial of the presence of God' revealed in the marvellous victory.

Against these charitable readings Matthew Henry sets a sterner one, and the witnesses let the two stand in tension. Henry sees a man 'turning aside from God, and yet now beginning to build altars, being most zealous, as many are, for the form of godliness when he was denying the power of it.' He hears the echo of the prophet's complaint: 'Israel hath forgotten his Maker, and buildeth temples' (Hosea 8:14). The debate is finally about Saul's heart, not his masonry. Was the altar a true thanksgiving, a memorial of God's presence and a public confession of His mercy? Or was it the reflex of a hollow piety, busy with sacred forms while the inward substance drained away? The commentators do not force a verdict, and perhaps the man himself was divided. But the placing of this first altar — hard beside the rash oath and the coming attempt on Jonathan's life — invites the reader to weigh whether zeal for the outward forms of worship can ever excuse a will that has ceased to bend to God.
36:
Flushed with success, Saul would press the slaughter through the night. 'Let us go down after the Philistines by night, and spoil them until the morning light, and let us not leave a man of them.' Matthew Henry hears in this 'much zeal, but little discretion,' for the army 'thus fatigued could as ill spare a night's sleep as a meal's meat.' The people, ever pliant, answer only, 'Do whatsoever seemeth good unto thee.' Charles Ellicott paints the grim scene the king intends: 'in the depth of the night' he 'would have hurried after the flying Philistines, and with a wild butchery have completed the great victory.'

The turn of the verse is the priest's quiet resistance. 'Then said the priest, Let us draw near hither unto God.' Keil and Delitzsch note that while the people assented, 'the priest wished first of all to obtain the decision of God.' Adam Clarke reads Ahiah's motive as caution: he 'doubted the propriety of pursuing that night,' knowing 'a reverse of fortune might be ruinous after such a victory.' Ellicott makes the priest the lone brave voice: 'only one man in that army flushed with victory dared, with the bravery which alone proceeds from righteousness, to withstand the imperious sovereign,' doubting whether 'such a wholesale bloodshed' accorded with God's will, since 'no command to exterminate these Philistines had ever been given.'

Matthew Henry draws the pastoral point from Ahiah's intervention: 'Princes and great men have need of such about them as will thus be their remembrancers, wherever they go, to take God along with them.' The witnesses commend the priest and, gently, expose the king. Saul's instinct is to act first and, if pressed, consult God after; Ahiah's is to seek God before the sword is drawn. When the priest proposes it, Henry notes, 'Saul could not for shame reject the proposal,' and so inquires — but the reader has already seen, at v.19, how little patience Saul has for an answer that does not come at once. The verse thus sets two spirits side by side: the ruler's restless zeal to finish the work by his own reckoning, and the priest's reverent pause to ask whether God wills it at all. It is the priest, not the king, who speaks here for true religion, and the chapter is about to show which of them read the mind of God aright.
37:
Saul consults the oracle, and heaven is silent. 'And Saul asked counsel of God, Shall I go down after the Philistines? wilt thou deliver them into the hand of Israel? But he answered him not that day.' Keil and Delitzsch read the silence as a summons to self-examination: Saul 'was to perceive from this that the guilt of some sin was resting upon the people, on account of which the Lord had turned away His countenance.' Charles Ellicott pictures the moment: 'when the mysterious gems refused to shine, or in any way to signify the Divine approbation,' the high priest 'concluded that some public transgression had been committed, and that special atonement must be made before the desired answer could be expected.'

The witnesses agree that the unanswered prayer is itself a message, and Matthew Henry presses the doctrine home: 'When God denies our prayers, it concerns us to enquire what the sin is that has provoked him to do so.' For 'God's ear is not heavy that it cannot hear, but it is sin that separates between us and him'; 'if God turns away our prayer, we have reason to suspect it is for some iniquity regarding our hearts, which we are concerned to find out, that we may put it away.'

Yet Adam Clarke enters a striking dissent about the nature of the 'sin.' 'Why was this answer delayed?' he asks. 'Surely Jonathan's eating the honey was no sin; this could not have excited God's displeasure.' Clarke argues that God withheld His answer 'because he did not think it proper to send the people by night in pursuit,' Saul's motive being 'perfectly vindictive'; and he points besides to the whole army's defilement in eating flesh with the blood, which left 'multitudes of them unclean.' On this reading the silence rebukes not Jonathan but Saul's bloodthirsty haste and the people's fresh transgression. The witnesses thus hold a real difference: Keil and Henry take the silence to point, providentially, toward the culprit the lot will name, while Clarke insists the true offence lay in Saul's vengeful design and the camp's uncleanness. All agree on the governing truth — that a God who will not answer is a God calling His people to search themselves — but they divide over whose sin it was that shut heaven's mouth that day.
38:
Saul convenes an inquiry to find the hidden fault. 'Draw ye near hither, all the chief of the people: and know and see wherein this sin hath been this day.' John Gill weighs the phrase 'chief of the people,' literally 'the corners of the peoples' — the 'princes,' as Jarchi has it, in allusion to the cornerstones 'which are the ornament, strength, and cement' of a building; though Abarbanel would read the tribes themselves. Gill adds the sober caveat that 'the tribes of Israel were not now present with Saul, but a small number of them.'
