Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch confirm the sense of the word pinnoth as 'corner stones' (compare Judges 20:2), the leading men who bear the weight of the nation. Ellicott sets the scene with feeling: 'round that rough unfinished altar, in the dark night, King Saul hastily summoned his leading officers and the prominent chiefs,' meaning 'to ask God's help in the casting of lots, to discover who was the transgressor, whose sin made dumb the Divine Oracle.'

The witnesses read the verse as the hinge between the silence of heaven and the trial about to unfold. Saul rightly infers, as the priest had taught him, that a sin lies somewhere in the camp; his instinct to seek it out is not wrong. But the reader, who has watched the whole day, already knows two things the king does not admit: that the likeliest 'sin' the lot will fasten upon is Jonathan's innocent taste of honey, and that the graver fault — the rash oath itself — sits unexamined upon the very man conducting the inquiry. There is a grim irony in Saul gathering the cornerstones of Israel by night to hunt a transgressor, when the transgression that troubled the day was his own curse. The verse shows a king earnest to purge a sin from his people and wholly unable to see it in himself; and it prepares the dreadful moment when his zeal for justice will turn upon the son who won him the victory. Gill and the grammatical witnesses read the summons plainly; the weight of the verse lies in what it foreshadows.
39:
Saul binds the inquiry with a terrible oath. 'As the LORD liveth, which saveth Israel, though it be in Jonathan my son, he shall surely die. But there was not a man among all the people that answered him.' John Gill marks the solemn form, sworn by the God 'which saveth Israel' — the very deliverance of the day invoked to seal a vow that will threaten its human author. Keil and Delitzsch render the emphatic Hebrew: 'even if it were upon my son, that he shall die,' the repetition giving the words their dreadful weight; and 'of all the people no one answered him, from terror at the king's word.'

The witnesses read the people's silence with one accord as protective love. John Wesley explains that 'none of those who saw Jonathan eating informed against him, because they were satisfied that his ignorance excused him, and from their great love to Jonathan, whom they would not expose to death for so small an offence.' The army knows the culprit and knows he is guiltless in heart; their hush is not fear only but a quiet refusal to hand over the man who had wrought their salvation.

Charles Ellicott lets Ewald capture the pathos of the scene: Saul, 'feeling inwardly bound by his oath, presses for decision by means of the sacred lot, amid the ominous silence of the horror-stricken people.' The commentators feel the tragic machinery closing. Saul's oath is doubly rash — first the fast that troubled the land, and now this vow that stakes a life on the outcome of a lot — and both will recoil upon his own house. He swears by the God who saves Israel that the saviour of Israel shall die if the lot so fall; he cannot see that the two halves of his sentence are at war. The people's silence is the first crack in the king's authority: they will obey his word in the pursuit and the sacrifice, but when it turns against Jonathan they answer nothing, and the reader senses already that this oath, unlike the last, they will not let him keep. Zeal for a vow has carried Saul to the brink of shedding the most precious blood in his kingdom.
40:
Saul divides the camp for the casting of lots. 'Be ye on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on the other side.' John Gill notes that the summons now reaches beyond the officers to the whole company — 'not the principal men only, but the common people; not the officers only, but the common soldiers in the army' — and again the people answer with the pliant formula they have used all day: 'Do what seemeth good unto thee.'

The witnesses observe the strange confidence with which Saul places himself and Jonathan together over against all Israel. As Matthew Henry remarks in surveying the scene, Saul does this 'perhaps because he was as confident of Jonathan's innocency in this matter as of his own.' There is a terrible irony in the arrangement: the king, sure that neither he nor his son can be the culprit, stakes them both against the multitude, and so narrows the lot, by his own hand, toward the very outcome he cannot imagine. The people acquiesce not from conviction but from dread, unwilling to 'gainsay any thing he proposed' in his heat.

Beneath the procedure the commentators find a meditation on the solemnity of the lot and the impartiality it demands. Henry commends the air of even-handed justice in Saul's setting himself on the side of possible guilt: 'Judges should desire that truth may come out, whoever may suffer by it.' Yet the reader knows what Saul does not — that the truth about to come out will not be the guilt of a real sin but the exposure of an innocent taste, and that the impartial machinery of the lot is being driven by a partial and rash oath. The verse holds the two together: a genuine reverence for divine adjudication, and a king so blind to his own error that he confidently herds himself and his best-loved son toward the judgment. The stage is set, and Israel waits in silence to see where the finger of God will fall. Gill marks the widening of the summons and Henry the impartial form; together they frame a king unknowingly steering the lot toward his own hearth.
41:
Saul appeals to God for a true verdict, and the lot narrows toward his house. 'Therefore Saul said unto the LORD God of Israel, Give a perfect lot. And Saul and Jonathan were taken: but the people escaped.' The witnesses debate the exact words of the prayer. John Wesley and Charles Ellicott both prefer the margin's 'show the innocent' — 'declare the perfect, or guiltless person,' as Wesley paraphrases, 'so guide the lot that it may discover who is guilty and who innocent.' Keil and Delitzsch render it 'Give innocence,' that is, truth. Ellicott, citing Payne Smith, defends the King James rendering as resting on Kimchi's authority even where the translators 'neglected the still higher authority of the ancient versions.'

The grammatical-historical commentators note that the Greek and Latin texts expand the prayer considerably. Adam Clarke reports the fuller form from the Vulgate and Septuagint: 'Lord God of Israel, give judgment. Why is it that thou hast not answered thy servant today? If the iniquity be in me, or Jonathan my son, make it manifest; but if this iniquity be in thy people, give sanctification.' Keil sets out the long Alexandrian paraphrase at length, though Ellicott judges that it 'contains no fact of additional interest.' The substance is plain across all the versions: Saul asks God to point out the guilty, and the lot falls upon Saul and Jonathan, the people going free.

Matthew Henry lifts the practice into doctrine. 'Lots should be cast with prayer, because they are a solemn appeal to Providence, and by them we beg of God to direct and determine us' (Acts 1:24). The witnesses honour the reverence of the act — Saul does not treat the lot as chance but as the voice of God — while feeling the pathos of its result. The king who prayed for a 'perfect lot' has his prayer answered against himself and his son. There is a somber fitness in it: heaven, which had kept silence all day, now speaks, and its first word draws the circle around the very man who bound Israel with the oath. The commentators leave the reader poised between two truths: the lot is genuinely God's answer, faithfully given to a sincere appeal; and what it exposes is not the sin Saul imagined in others but the ruin his own rashness has prepared for his house.
42:
The lot is cast a second time, between father and son, and it falls on Jonathan. John Gill reads Saul's readiness to include himself as a show of even-handedness: it 'showed his regard to strict justice, and that he had no consciousness of guilt in himself, and should not spare his own son if found guilty.' John Wesley, however, insists that the lot's fall is God's doing with a purpose quite other than Saul's: God 'so ordered the lot, not that he approved Saul's execration, nor that he would expose Jonathan to death; but that Saul's folly might be chastised, when he saw what danger it had brought upon his eldest and excellent son, and that Jonathan's innocency might be cleared.'

Adam Clarke presses the crucial distinction that the lot names the actor but not a sinner. 'The object of the inquiry most evidently was, Who has gone contrary to the king's adjuration today? The answer to that must be Jonathan.' Yet 'was this a proof of the Divine displeasure against the man? By no means: the holy oracle told the truth, but neither that oracle nor the God who gave it fixed any blame upon Jonathan, and his own conscience acquits him.' Clarke adds his own explanation of why God had earlier kept silence — 'because he did not think it proper to send the people by night' on so 'vindictive' an errand, and because the camp was defiled with blood — so that the lot exposes a fact without endorsing Saul's sentence.

The witnesses thus reach a careful and united verdict beneath their differing emphases. The lot is truthful: Jonathan did taste the honey, and God's providence unerringly points to him. But truth is not the same as guilt. Gill sees Saul's justice, Clarke sees Jonathan's innocence, and Wesley sees God's design to shame the father through the peril of the son — and all three agree that the God who guided the lot did not thereby pronounce Jonathan a criminal. The commentators guard here against a dangerous misreading: that whatever the lot or providence singles out must be under divine condemnation. Rather, God can use even a rash king's machinery to bring a hidden matter to light, to chastise the folly that framed the oath, and to prepare the vindication of the innocent. The finger of God has fallen on Jonathan, but it points past him to the father whose vow has brought his house to this pass.
43:
Called to account, Jonathan tells the plain truth and bows to its consequence. 'I did but taste a little honey with the end of the rod that was in mine hand, and, lo, I must die.' Keil and Delitzsch mark the quiet resignation: Jonathan 'confessed that he had tasted a little honey, and resigned himself to the punishment suspended over him, saying, Behold, I shall die.' John Gill notes only that Saul demanded to know 'what sin he had committed, the lot having found him out.'

The witnesses dwell on the greatness of soul in Jonathan's answer. As Matthew Henry observes, Jonathan 'denies not the truth, nor goes about to conceal it,' though 'he might very fairly have pleaded his invincible ignorance of the law, or have insisted upon his merit.' Instead he 'submitted to the necessity with a great and generous mind,' as if to say, 'God's and my father's will be done.' Henry finds in this the same valour that stormed the cliff: 'thus he showed as much valour in receiving the messengers of death himself as in sending them among the Philistines. It is as brave to yield in some cases as it is in other cases to fight.'

The commentators let the contrast between father and son speak. The whole verse turns on the little words 'a little honey' set against 'I must die' — the triviality of the deed against the enormity of the threatened penalty, and the reader is meant to feel the disproportion as an indictment of the oath, not of the man. Jonathan does not argue, though every argument was on his side; he simply confesses and submits, laying his life open with the same wholehearted courage he had shown on Bozez. There is something Christlike in the picture the witnesses draw: the deliverer of Israel, guiltless of any real wrong, standing silent under an unjust sentence and yielding himself to his father's word. The honey that opened his eyes has become the occasion of his peril, and the man who saved the nation now waits, uncomplaining, to see whether the nation will save him.
44:
Saul pronounces the dreadful sentence, sealing it with yet another oath. 'God do so and more also: for thou shalt surely die, Jonathan.' John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch note the familiar formula of self-imprecation, 'God do so and more also,' the same solemn curse used elsewhere in Scripture (compare Ruth 1:17). Gill records that Abarbanel would soften it into a mere asseveration, but the witnesses generally take it as a binding vow that Saul now turns against his own son.

Adam Clarke erupts in indignation at the sentence. 'To save thy rash oath!' he exclaims; 'so must John the Baptist's head be taken off at the desire of an impure woman, because a Herod had sworn to give her whatever she might request! Unfeeling brute!' Then, recovering the legal frame, he adds: 'However, the king was Judge. But what said the people, who were the Jury?' — setting up the verdict of the next verse. Matthew Henry anatomizes the fault with equal severity: Saul 'passed this sentence too hastily, without consulting the oracle,' and 'did it in fury,' when it would have become 'a father to pass sentence with tenderness and compassion.' 'Justice is debased,' Henry writes, 'when it is administered with wrath and bitterness.'

The witnesses agree that the curse recoils upon its author. Henry observes that Saul 'backed it with a curse upon himself if he did not see the sentence executed; and this curse did return upon his own head. Jonathan escaped, but God did so to Saul, and more also; for he was rejected of God and made anathema.' He draws the warning broad: 'Let none upon any occasion dare to use such imprecations as these, lest God say Amen to them; this stone will return upon him that rolleth it.' The commentators see in Saul's sentence the full flowering of the chapter's central sin — a will so wedded to its own word that it would sooner slay the saviour of Israel than confess a rash vow. The father who should have shielded his son now condemns him to keep a curse; and the God who guided the lot will, in His own time, turn that curse back upon the head that swore it.
45:
The people rise as one to save Jonathan, matching the king's oath with their own. 'Shall Jonathan die, who hath wrought this great salvation in Israel? God forbid: as the LORD liveth, there shall not one hair of his head fall to the ground; for he hath wrought with God this day.' Keil and Delitzsch judge the objection unanswerable: 'The objection raised by the people was so conclusive that Saul was obliged to yield. What Jonathan had done was not wrong in itself, but became so simply on account of the oath, which Jonathan did not hear, and therefore had not even consciously transgressed.'

Adam Clarke frames the rescue in the language of a courtroom he began at v.44: 'Here was a righteous and impartial jury, who brought in a verdict according to the evidence: No man should die but for a breach of the law of God; but Jonathan hath not broken any law of God; therefore Jonathan should not die.' The phrase 'he hath wrought with God this day' is, for the witnesses, the heart of the plea. John Wesley expounds it: Jonathan wrought 'in concurrence with God'; and 'God is so far from being offended with Jonathan, that he hath graciously owned him in the great service of this day.' The very silence of the oracle, on this reading, condemned not the son but the whole vengeful project.

Matthew Henry celebrates the people's holy defiance. 'When Jonathan is in danger, Saul's word is no longer a law to them, but with the utmost zeal they oppose the execution of his sentence.' They 'oppose their oath to his, and swear he shall not die' — 'they did not rescue him by violence, but by reason and resolution,' and, as Josephus adds, 'made their prayer to God that he might be loosed from the curse.' The witnesses read the scene as the vindication both of Jonathan and of God's own verdict. The man the lot had singled out, the man his father had condemned, is declared by the whole army to have 'wrought with God' — and the people's judgment proves truer than the king's. It is a striking moment: a rash sovereign overruled, an innocent saviour delivered, and the common voice of Israel becoming, for once, the instrument of righteousness against the very throne. Henry's approval is warm: 'It is good to see Israelites zealous for the protection of those whom God has made instruments of public good.'
46:
The night pursuit is abandoned, and the enemy slips away. 'Then Saul went up from following the Philistines: and the Philistines went to their own place.' Keil and Delitzsch read the retreat as the fruit of a guilty conscience: 'With the feeling of this guilt, Saul gave up any further pursuit,' desisting and going up to Gibeah, while the Philistines withdrew 'back into their own land.' Charles Ellicott agrees that Saul 'recognised now that the fault which caused the oracle to keep silence was his, and not Jonathan's,' and so 'quietly acquiesced with Ahijah's evident reluctance to countenance a public pursuit.'

John Gill notes the plainer cause — Saul returned home 'finding that he could get no answer from the Lord,' having 'lost the time and opportunity' while examining Jonathan and casting lots. The witnesses thus see the whole tragic economy of the day: the rash oath had starved the army, the starving had bred the sin of the blood, the sin had shut the oracle, and the closed oracle had spent the night on an inquiry that let the Philistines escape. One folly at dawn had unravelled the victory by dusk.

Matthew Henry draws the sobering conclusion. 'The design against the Philistines is quashed by this incident, and so an opportunity was lost of completing the victory.' He turns it to a lasting lesson on the cost of division within the people of God: 'When Israel's shields are clashing with one another, the public safety and service suffer by it.' The commentators let the chapter's great deliverance close on this note of loss. God had given a stunning victory through Jonathan's faith; Saul's rashness had squandered its fullness. The enemy that might have been destroyed 'walks quietly home,' and the reader is left to weigh how a single self-willed vow, laid across a day of grace, can dissipate the very triumph heaven had won. The salvation was real and remains; but its completeness was forfeited, and the forfeiture was the king's, whose one self-willed vow at dawn had spent, by dusk, the very triumph heaven had won. The salvation stood; only its fullness was lost, and lost needlessly.
47:
The narrative lifts from the single day to survey the whole reign. 'So Saul took the kingdom over Israel, and fought against all his enemies on every side.' The witnesses divide over the phrase 'took the kingdom.' Keil and Delitzsch read it strongly: through this victory over the Philistines, 'by which these obstinate foes were driven back into their own land,' Saul 'first really secured the regal authority over Israel,' a statement they hold consistent with his earlier election by lot (1 Samuel 10:20) and confirmation at Gilgal (1 Samuel 11:14–15). John Gill agrees he 'recovered his kingdom,' or, following Kimchi, 'was now strengthened in it,' and cites Abarbanel that Saul now 'took upon him the state and majesty of a king,' leaving his herds for 'regal and military affairs.'

Charles Ellicott prefers to take the verse as a general heading to the reign, 'a reign of perpetual wars,' introducing 'the list of wars waged from the very beginning of his government' — a reading he supports by the mention of the Ammonite war, which fell earlier (1 Samuel 11:12). He maps the foes on every side: 'Moab and Ammon bounded the Israelites on the east; Edom on the south; the Philistines on the west; while Zobah was a district of Syria on the north-east.'

A final crux attends the closing word, 'he vexed them.' Charles Ellicott confesses it 'has puzzled all commentators': the Septuagint 'evidently read another word,' translating 'he was preserved,' while Gesenius and the majority give 'whithersoever he turned himself he was victorious,' and Luther's scholarly rendering, adopted by Keil, is 'whithersoever he turned he inflicted punishment.' Matthew Henry gathers the sense simply: Saul 'guarded his country against the insults of its enemies on every side,' and 'whithersoever he turned himself, he vexed them, by checking and disappointing them.' Beneath the philology the witnesses agree on the picture: the king whose rashness had just marred a victory is nonetheless established by God as Israel's defender, and for a season the fortunes of the nation rise. The verse holds together the mystery of Saul — a flawed and faltering man, yet the instrument through whom, for a time, God pushed back the enemies of His people on every hand.
48:
Among all Saul's wars, one is singled out. 'And he gathered an host, and smote the Amalekites, and delivered Israel out of the hands of them that spoiled them.' The witnesses note first a small translational difference. Charles Ellicott observes that the English 'he gathered an host' follows the Syriac and Vulgate, but 'the marginal translation, he wrought mightily, is the more accurate.' Keil and Delitzsch agree the Hebrew denotes 'the development and unfolding of power in various respects,' here 'the development of strength in the war against Amalek.'

The commentators agree on why this campaign is named ahead of the others. Adam Clarke notes simply that 'this war is mentioned in the following chapter,' and Ellicott explains that 'out of the many wars the king waged, this war with Amalek is singled out,' being 'especially prominent' in 'the new development of Hebrew power' under Saul. Keil points to its graver significance: it is 'described more fully in the next chapter, on account of its consequences in relation to Saul's own sovereignty' (1 Samuel 15).

The witnesses let the verse cast a long shadow forward. Here the Amalekite war appears only as one bright thread in the tapestry of Saul's victories — he 'delivered Israel out of the hands of them that spoiled them,' the ancient oppressor smitten, the people rescued. But every commentator knows what the reader will soon learn: that this very war, so creditable in summary, will become the occasion of Saul's final rejection, when his incomplete obedience in sparing Agag and the spoil draws Samuel's sentence, 'to obey is better than sacrifice.' The narrator's placing of the notice is thus quietly ominous. The chapter that has shown Saul's rashness threaten his son now records, in a single approving line, the campaign that will undo his throne. The witnesses read it as a hinge: a genuine deliverance wrought for Israel, and, hidden within it, the seed of the king's own fall — the very sword-arm that here rescues Israel will there, through incomplete obedience in sparing Agag and the spoil, cost Saul his very throne.
49:
The record turns to Saul's household. 'Now the sons of Saul were Jonathan, and Ishui, and Melchishua: and the names of his two daughters were these; the name of the firstborn Merab, and the name of the younger Michal.' Keil and Delitzsch explain that 'only three of his sons are mentioned, namely those who fell with him' at Gilboa (1 Samuel 31:2), and that 'Ishui is only another name for Abinadab' (compare 1 Chronicles 8:33). The absence of Ish-bosheth, later set up by Abner as David's rival, they leave undetermined — perhaps 'the name has fallen out through some mistake in copying.'

The witnesses largely agree on the omission and its likely reason. John Wesley notes that 'Ishui is called also Abinadab,' and that 'Ishbosheth, Saul's other son, is here omitted, because the writer intended to mention only those of his sons who went with him into the battles here mentioned, and who were afterwards slain with him.' Adam Clarke raises the natural objection — 'why then mention his daughters and his wife? Did they go with him to the war?' — leaving the difficulty open, while citing Calmet that Ish-bosheth 'was too young' for these campaigns.

Charles Ellicott supplies the reason the daughters are named, and it points forward to the great story of the books of Samuel. Merab and Michal 'are specially named, probably owing to their connection with the history of David,' 'the elder of them having been promised to him in marriage, and the younger being actually wedded to him' (1 Samuel 18:17–21). The witnesses thus read this dry family register as anything but idle. Every name in it is a thread that the coming narrative will draw taut: Jonathan, whose covenant love for David will outshine his father's throne; Michal, who will love and then despise David; the sons who will die on Gilboa; the shadowed absence of the son who will contest the crown. The catalogue that seems to pause the action is in truth laying out the cast of the tragedy to come, and the witnesses honour the narrator's economy in setting it here, at the height of Saul's fortunes, just before the long decline begins.
50:
The household record continues with Saul's wife and his general. 'The name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam, the daughter of Ahimaaz: and the name of the captain of his host was Abner, the son of Ner, Saul's uncle.' Keil and Delitzsch clarify the family tie: 'Abner the general was also Saul's cousin,' and for 'son of Abiel' we should read 'sons of Abiel' (compare 1 Samuel 9:1), Kish and Ner being brothers. John Gill supposes Ahinoam 'was the mother of all the above children,' and notes that though Saul had a concubine, 'we nowhere read of his having another wife.'

Charles Ellicott reflects on the modesty of the royal house. 'We know nothing of Saul's queen besides her name,' and 'the simplicity of the king's domestic habits is evident'; Ewald infers that Saul 'had only this one wife and one concubine, Rizpah,' whose sad devotion to her children is told later (2 Samuel 21:8–12). Ellicott even surmises, from the recurring 'Ah' in the names, that Ahinoam may have belonged 'to the family of Eli, the high priest.'

The greatest interest gathers on Abner. Charles Ellicott judges him 'evidently a man of rare powers and ability,' to whom 'the brilliant campaigns of this reign were, no doubt, in no small measure owing.' He carries the thought to its poignant end: after the disaster on Mount Gilboa, Abner 'was the mainstay of the house of the dead King Saul, and when he died the generous David followed the bier, and lamented over him' — 'Know ye not that there is a prince and a great man fallen this day in Israel.' The witnesses read the verse, once again, as a quiet seedbed of the larger history. The queen whose name barely survives, the concubine whose grief will one day move a king, the great captain who will hold Saul's house together and then fall to treachery — all are set down here in a line or two, at the summit of Saul's power, and every one of them belongs to the sorrow that is coming. The narrator names them now so that the reader, meeting them again in their griefs, will know whose house it was that rose so high and sank so far.
51:
A brief genealogical note rounds out the family list. 'And Kish was the father of Saul; and Ner the father of Abner was the son of Abiel.' The witnesses treat it chiefly as a matter of textual precision. Charles Ellicott observes that 'for son the commentators mostly agree we must read sons,' since 'Kish and Ner were both sons of Abiel' (compare 1 Chronicles 9:35–36, 'where, however, the father's name is given as Jehiel'). Keil and Delitzsch concur, harmonizing the verse with the genealogy of 1 Samuel 9:1.

The apparent tangle — one verse calling Ner the son of Abiel, another passage naming Jehiel as the father — is the kind of small discrepancy the grammatical-historical witnesses take pains to smooth. Their solution is straightforward: Abiel and Jehiel are variant forms or the same ancestor differently rendered, and 'son' here stands loosely for 'descendant,' as Hebrew genealogy often allows. The point they wish to secure is the kinship that binds the royal house together: Saul the king and Abner his commander are cousins, both sprung from the same Benjamite stock through Kish and Ner, the sons of Abiel.

The witnesses do not moralize heavily over so terse a verse, but they let it perform its structural work. The genealogy fastens Saul and Abner together at the root, so that the reader understands the reign as very much a family affair — a king and a general who are kinsmen, the fortunes of a single Benjamite clan lifted for a time to the throne of Israel. When that house comes to grief, its griefs will be domestic as well as national; the war between Saul's line and David's will be, in part, the ruin of this one family whose pedigree is here so carefully set down. The commentators' patient reconciling of Abiel and Jehiel, of 'son' and 'sons,' honours the narrator's care to fix exactly who these men were, that the great story about to unfold might rest on a firm and traceable ground of fact. Where the record seems merely to list names, the witnesses find both a knot of kinship and a promise of tragedy quietly tied.
52:
The chapter ends where the reign will spend itself — in unending war. 'There was sore war against the Philistines all the days of Saul: and when Saul saw any strong man, or any valiant man, he took him unto him.' John Gill notes that despite the great victory at Michmash, the Philistines 'recovered themselves, and came out again to battle,' giving Saul 'a great deal of trouble,' until 'he at last died in battle with them.' Keil and Delitzsch read the two clauses together: because Saul 'had to carry on a severe war against the Philistines his whole life long, he drew to himself every powerful man and every brave man that he met with.'

Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott admire the policy of it. Clarke calls the recruiting 'very politic,' by which Saul 'continued to recruit his army with strong and effective men.' Ellicott notes that the abiding Philistine menace 'necessitated the maintenance of a standing army, which, in the days of David, became one of the great armed forces of the East,' and compares Saul's eye for strong men to 'a similar military fancy of King Frederick William of Prussia, the founder of Prussian military greatness.' The verse also, in Matthew Henry's reading, fulfils Samuel's warning of 'the manner of the king' who 'will take your sons' (1 Samuel 8:11).

Matthew Henry closes the chapter on a note of sober pathos, and the witnesses let his word stand as the summing-up. Saul 'had little reason to be proud of his royal dignity, nor had any of his neighbours cause to envy him, for he had little enjoyment of himself after he took the kingdom. He could not vex his enemies without some vexation to himself; such thorns are crowns quilted with.' The commentators end, therefore, not in triumph but in shadow. The chapter that opened with a dazzling deliverance closes with a lifetime of grinding warfare and a king forever scanning the ranks for stronger men to spend. Henry's final image lingers: 'often men's earthly glory makes a blaze just before the dark night of disgrace and woe comes on them.' Saul stands here at his zenith — kingdom secured, enemies vexed on every side, an army of the mighty gathering about him — and the witnesses, knowing what lies ahead, hear in the very grandeur the first low note of the coming tragedy.
